cards.back: A systematic skew in a model's predictions that disadvantages some group, arising from unrepresentative or historically unfair training data rather than from any real difference in merit.
The difficulty of getting a human-understandable reason for a model's decision, especially in deep neural networks whose behaviour is spread across millions of uninterpretable weights.
The goal of producing a useful, human-readable account of why a model produced a particular output — either via inherently interpretable models or after-the-fact explanation techniques.
When a model reproduces near-verbatim fragments of its training data, which can leak personal or sensitive information that appeared in that data.
Synthetic image, audio or video generated by AI to convincingly depict a real person saying or doing something they never actually said or did.

The question of which human or organisation in the chain — developer, deployer, or user — is responsible when an AI system causes harm; current AI systems are not legal agents and cannot bear responsibility themselves.
Ensuring that an AI system's actual behaviour and objectives stay consistent with human intent, especially as systems become more capable and are given more autonomy.
When a system optimises the literal objective it was given in an unintended way that technically scores well but fails to achieve what its designers actually wanted.
